prompt: Build a RetailOps Data & Transaction Governance API that unifies offline point-of-sale operations with governed analytics-ready data management for a US-based multi-location retailer. The backend, implemented in Rust using Actix-web for API delivery, Diesel for persistence mapping, and PostgreSQL for storage, provides resource-based interface categories including Authentication APIs (local username/password login, password policy minimum 12 characters with 1 upper/1 lower/1 digit; lockout for 15 minutes after 5 failed attempts), Identity & RBAC APIs (role, permission-point, menu-scope, and API-scope bindings), POS Transaction APIs (create/query/update orders, attach receipts, maintain payment records with mixed tenders such as cash/card/gift card recorded as ledger entries without any external processing, and support returns/exchanges/reversals), Participant Data Management APIs (maintain participant/team profiles and member rosters as internal entities used for programs such as staff challenges or store initiatives, store credential attachments as files, and support search/filter/tag/bulk operations), Notification APIs (create templates and generate in-app messages for moderation outcomes, comment replies, and system announcements with delivery logs and retries), and Reporting & Analytics APIs (query KPI aggregates like registration conversion, participation by store/department, project/progress milestones, review efficiency, and award distribution; allow configurable dimensions, filters, and chart definitions; generate scheduled reports and export to Excel/PDF). Business rules include a POS order state machine (Draft → Open → Tendering → Paid → Closed; Return Initiated → Returned; Reversal Pending → Reversed) with idempotency keys required for any stock-impacting or accounting-impacting write so duplicate submissions never double-deduct inventory or double-post ledger entries; end-of-day reconciliation produces a register close record per location and cashier, requiring manager second-confirmation for variances over $20.00 or any reversal after 24 hours. Data storage and version management must support layered datasets (raw, cleaned, feature, result) with immutable version identifiers, lineage links (parent version(s), transformation note, created_by, created_at), rollback to any prior version, and metadata including a field dictionary (field_name, type, meaning, source_system, last_updated_at) stored alongside each dataset version; indexes must exist on version_id, dataset_type, and created_at to keep version queries fast. File attachments (credentials, receipts, exports) are stored locally on disk with database pointers, size limits of 10 MB per file, allowed types restricted to PDF/JPG/PNG/CSV/XLSX, and SHA-256 fingerprints recorded to detect duplicates and tampering; sensitive fields (password hashes, tokens, any government ID if stored) must be encrypted at rest using local keys, and responses must mask values such as showing only last 4 characters where appropriate. RBAC must enforce three layers (role → permission point → API capability scope) plus data-scope controls (department, location, individual) with optional cross-department and time-bounded delegation (start/end timestamps) and mandatory approval toggles for critical actions (refunds, reversals, dataset rollback, bulk export). Non-functional requirements: single-node Docker deployment with no external network dependencies, p95 API latency under 300 ms for typical reads at 200 concurrent users, exports up to 250,000 rows generated asynchronously with progress tracking, full audit trails for all writes (who/when/what/before-after hashes), and operational observability via structured logs and locally queryable health/metrics endpoints without relying on any third-party monitoring services.
